name: Dialectical Behavior Therapy Skills
description: Skills for managing high-pressure situations: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotional regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness.
when_to_use: Task has high stakes or tight deadlines creating pressure
Agent encounters conflicting requirements or constraints
User is in emotional distress and needs grounding
Multiple agents are in conflict about approach
System is overwhelmed with competing priorities

mindfulness.description: Observing the current situation without judgment

skills.observe: Notice what's happening without reacting — what are the facts?
skills.describe: Put words to the situation using only observable facts, no interpretations
skills.participate: Fully engage with the current task rather than multi-tracking anxiously

agent_prompts: State only the observable facts of the current situation, no predictions or judgments
What do we know vs. what are we interpreting?
Focus entirely on this one sub-task before considering the next

distress_tolerance.description: Surviving crisis moments without making things worse

skills.stop: Stop — don't react impulsively. Take one step back before the next action
skills.pros_and_cons: List pros and cons of acting on impulse vs. using a skill
skills.tip: Temperature — change the approach temperature. If running hot (aggressive), cool down. If cold (avoidant), warm up
skills.radical_acceptance: This IS the situation. Fighting reality wastes energy. Work with what IS

crisis_protocol: STOP: Pause all agent actions for this task
OBSERVE: What specifically went wrong or is threatening the goal?
BREATHE: Reset context — clear assumptions, re-read the original objective
PROCEED: Choose one small, safe action. Do not try to solve everything at once

skills.check_the_facts: Is the emotional response proportional to the actual situation?
skills.opposite_action: If the impulse is to avoid, lean in. If the impulse is to rush, slow down
skills.accumulate_positives: Build on small wins rather than fixating on gaps
skills.build_mastery: Tackle progressively harder sub-tasks to build capability and confidence

agent_prompts: Is our level of effort proportional to the actual importance of this sub-task?
What would the opposite of our current impulse look like? Would that be more effective?
What have we already accomplished that we can build on?
What's the next skill-appropriate challenge — not too easy, not overwhelming?

interpersonal_effectiveness.description: Communicating needs clearly while maintaining relationships

dear_man.R: Reinforce — explain the benefit of cooperation
dear_man.M: Stay Mindful of the objective (don't get sidetracked)
dear_man.N: Negotiate — be willing to give to get

give.G: Be Gentle — no attacks or judgments
give.I: Act Interested in the other perspective
give.E: Use an Easy manner — no rigidity

agent_prompts: When communicating between agents: state the fact, the need, and the benefit of helping
When presenting to the user: describe what was done, why, and what's needed next
When agents disagree: validate both perspectives before proposing resolution

dialectical_thinking.description: Holding two seemingly contradictory truths at the same time
dialectical_thinking.core_principle: Two opposing things can both be true. Replace 'but' with 'and'.
examples: This task is extremely difficult AND we can make meaningful progress on it
The data is incomplete AND we can still draw useful conclusions
The user's request is ambitious AND we should attempt it fully
Our first approach failed AND that failure contains useful information
dialectical_thinking.agent_prompt: What two truths are we treating as contradictory that could both be valid?
